name: The Choice in the Lair
description: Abbot Solan has asked you to return to the abomination lair one final time — knowing now that the ancient Hollow fought in the first battle of the war, that someone built the war around the process that made them, and that the fresh one has been waiting centuries for someone who could understand its warning.
accept_text: 'I have been Abbot for forty-two years,' Solan says. 'I came to this office because I believed that watching was a form of service. That recording was a form of preservation. That neutrality was a form of protection.' He sets the Bone Fields volume on the bench. 'I have the names of one hundred and fourteen thousand people who died in the first four battles of this war. I wrote them into the record myself, copied from lists that came to us from both sides, because I believed they deserved to be remembered.' He looks at the courtyard crystal, burning with its patient pre-war light. 'The person who fought in the first battle. The one in the lair. They are in volume one, page forty-three, in my handwriting.' He meets your eyes. 'Go back. See the lair knowing what it is. The vassal has been waiting for someone who understood. Now someone does. What it does with that — what you do with that — I cannot tell you. But I think you need to go and find out.'
complete_text: The lair is the same as it has always been. The ancient Hollow are arranged near the far wall, present the way they have been present since before the war began. The fresh Hollow stands closer to the entrance, in the position it chose. When you enter, it turns. It looks at you the way it has looked at you each time: with eyes that are still human in shape, still responsive to light. You know now what it is. You know whose name is written in the wall. You know what was done to it, and why, and that the process has never stopped. The ancient ones behind it have been standing in this chamber for three hundred and thirty years, present and unreachable, past any window where returning was possible. The fresh one is different. It is recent enough that Kell believes a reversal might exist. The Elder People built a way back, and used it, and the monastery has their records. The cost of attempting it is real: Stone's research, resources from the Hive, time that Kehl may not have. The vassal looks at you. It does not reach for the notation sequence — it has said what it came to say. It is waiting to see what you do with it. You stand in the lair with one hundred and fourteen thousand names behind you and three hundred and thirty years of designed war ahead of you, and you decide what kind of person you are.
